title: Proxmox QEMU/KVM Live Migration Task Failure Detection
exclusions: This use case is scoped to PVE `qmigrate` (QEMU/KVM online live migration) and optional companion HTTP context from pveproxy access logs, not to Proxmox **vzdump** backup job outcomes (UC-19.1.37), Ceph `HEALTH_*` or OSD placement (UC-19.1.39), or **pvesr** per-disk replication job rows (UC-19.1.40). It excludes cold relocation paths such as `qm move` for stopped VMs, offline `pct migrate` for containers, and `move_disk` when only storage moves but the running guest does not change nodes. LXC `lxcmigrate` is restart-based, not pre-copy online migration—monitor it with a separate sourcetype or a sibling UC if you need parity. Hypervisor-wide “which node should this guest run on” schedulers, VMware DRS, Nutanix automatic placement, and similar policy engines are out of scope. Cross-product comparisons (oVirt, OpenStack live migrate, Hyper-V Live Migration) are not implied by the field names. vzdump- or restore-driven `qmrestore` task lines belong to backup/restore monitoring unless you split them by `type` in a dedicated poller. Planned simulation labs that intentionally break migrations for training are expected to create noise—govern with `index=lab` or a suppress tag rather than redefining production thresholds here.
evidence: Saved search `proxmox_qmigrate_task_failures_24h` (or your name) in `proxmox-monitoring`, scheduled every 5–15 minutes, plus a dashboard that lists `source_node`, `vmid`, `severity_bucket`, `reasons`, and `last_upid`, retained per your internal evidence policy.
dataSources: `index=virtualization` (or your site standard) with (1) `sourcetype=proxmox:task:qmigrate` — one minified JSON line per task row from scripted polling of `GET /api2/json/cluster/tasks?typefilter=qmigrate` (and optional per-node `.../nodes/{node}/tasks`) carrying `upid`, `node` (source), `id` (vmid string), `user`, `status` (`OK`, `running`, or error text), `starttime`/`endtime` epoch when present, and a flattened `target_node` if your poller can populate it; and (2) `sourcetype=proxmox:pveproxy:access` from `monitor:///var/log/pveproxy/access.log` for `POST` lines that include `/qemu/<vmid>/migrate` and `target=<dst>` query pairs. No Splunkbase TA ships a complete Proxmox PVE field mapping; you own an on-disk `proxmox-monitoring` (name yours) with `inputs.conf` + `props.conf`. Optional: tail `/var/log/pve/tasks/...` multiline data with a distinct sourcetype if you add regex-based `LINE_BREAKER` in a follow-on props stanza. Optional tertiary: `linux` `syslog` for kernel `migration` or bridge drops only when you can prove co-timestamps with the task `upid` window.
app: Custom `proxmox-monitoring` app (scripted `poll_qmigrate_tasks.py` + `inputs.conf` + `props.conf` + optional `lookups/proxmox_vm_inventory.csv`); optional Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux as `Splunk_TA_nix` ([Splunkbase 833](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/833), 9.x) when the same forwarder also ships `rsyslog` OS logs. There is no supported Splunkbase add-on that replaces the API poller; community packages are not a substitute for tested `PVEAPIToken` configuration.
spl:
index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:qmigrate" earliest=-24h@h
| eval source_node = coalesce(source_node, node, host)
| eval vmid = coalesce(vmid, id)
| eval status_norm = lower(coalesce(status, ""))
| where status_norm != "ok" AND status_norm != "running" AND isnotnull(status)
| eval reason_norm = case(
    match(status_norm, "(target host cpu does not support|cpu (model|feature)|incompatible cpu)"), "cpu_flag_mismatch",
    match(status_norm, "(could not migrate.*local|local-disk|local disk image|storage.*not (shared|available))"), "local_storage_unreachable",
    match(status_norm, "(channel disconnected|connection reset|network unreachable|migration network)"), "network_disconnect",
    match(status_norm, "(downtime|migration speed too slow|excessive (dirty|memory)|auto.?converge.*failed)"), "excessive_dirty_pages",
    match(status_norm, "(insufficient (memory|ram)|out of memory|storage.*full|max.*concurrent.*migrations)"), "target_resource_exhausted",
    match(status_norm, "(firewall|port.*blocked|connection refused.*60[0-9]{3})"), "firewall_blocked",
    match(status_norm, "(qmp command.*failed|migrate.set.capabilities|migrate.set.parameters)"), "qmp_command_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(local cd.?rom|iso (image|attached)|cdrom is on local)"), "iso_attached",
    match(status_norm, "(pci.?(e)? passthrough|hostpci|sr.?iov)"), "pci_passthrough_present",
    match(status_norm, "(corosync|quorum|cluster membership)"), "corosync_unhealthy",
    match(status_norm, "got timeout|operation timed out|deadline exceeded"), "timeout",
    1==1, "other_error")
| eval duration_sec = if(isnotnull(endtime) AND isnotnull(starttime), endtime - starttime, null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_failure,
        count as failures_24h,
        values(reason_norm) as reasons,
        values(status) as raw_status_msgs,
        values(target_node) as target_nodes,
        latest(duration_sec) as last_duration_sec,
        latest(upid) as last_upid,
        latest(user) as initiated_by
        by source_node, vmid
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    mvfind(reasons, "cpu_flag_mismatch") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "pci_passthrough_present") >= 0, "critical",
    failures_24h >= 3 AND mvfind(reasons, "network_disconnect") >= 0, "high",
    mvfind(reasons, "target_resource_exhausted") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "corosync_unhealthy") >= 0, "high",
    failures_24h >= 1, "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| sort -failures_24h, +severity_bucket
| table last_failure, source_node, target_nodes, vmid, severity_bucket, failures_24h, reasons, raw_status_msgs, last_duration_sec, initiated_by, last_upid
description: Consumes PVE `cluster/tasks` history rows in `sourcetype=proxmox:task:qmigrate` (scripted API poll) where `type` is `qmigrate`, normalises source and guest keys with `coalesce(source_node,node,host)` and `coalesce(vmid,id)`, lowercases the PVE `status` string to `status_norm`, filters out success (`ok`) and in-flight (`running`) rows, maps remaining text to `reason_norm` with `match()`-driven buckets (`cpu_flag_mismatch`, `local_storage_unreachable`, `network_disconnect`, `excessive_dirty_pages`, `target_resource_exhausted`, `firewall_blocked`, `qmp_command_failed`, `iso_attached`, `pci_passthrough_present`, `corosync_unhealthy`, `timeout`, else `other_error`), estimates `duration_sec` from `endtime`−`starttime`, aggregates a rolling 24h view per `source_node`+`vmid` with `stats` including `failures_24h`, `reasons`, and `initiated_by`, and assigns a `severity_bucket` where configuration-class failures and resource or membership pressure outrank a single blip. Optional `proxmox:pveproxy:access` provides who hit the migrate API when joined by `vmid` and time.
value: Rolling reboots, rack moves, and firmware windows depend on guests actually landing on a healthy node while operators still have room to re-run. A failed online migration that stays invisible until a second fault risks leadership believing the estate is already drained when half the guests are still on the old host, and it complicates RTO and change evidence when a maintenance ticket is already closed. Surfacing `qmigrate` failures with machine-class reasons, a durable `last_upid`, and repeat counts in 24h gives the platform and application teams a time-bounded path to retune CPU models, free RAM on the target, open migration firewall ranges, or remove ISO and passthrough blockers before a narrow window expires. The outcome is fewer stuck evacuations, cleaner hand-offs between SRE and storage or cluster admins, and an audit trail that ties each failed move to a concrete PVE task row and operator drilldown instead of a vague “the VM is slow” report.
implementation: Deploy `proxmox-monitoring` on a forwarder with HTTPS to the PVE API, place API token material in a restricted file, enable the `poll_qmigrate_tasks` scripted input to `sourcetype=proxmox:task:qmigrate`, add `monitor` for `pveproxy` access, register `proxmox_vm_inventory.csv` if you need owner routing, save the `spl` as a scheduled search with 5–15 minute cadence, throttle on `source_node`+`vmid`, and pack drilldowns with `https://<node>:8006/?#v1:0:tasks` plus raw-search tokens for the same `earliest` as `last_failure`.
visualization: (1) Single value: count of `severity_bucket` in {critical, high} for the time picker. (2) Table: `last_failure`, `source_node`, `target_nodes`, `vmid`, `severity_bucket`, `failures_24h`, `reasons`, `last_duration_sec`, `initiated_by`, `last_upid`, with drilldown to raw events and a child search over `proxmox:pveproxy:access` by `vmid`. (3) Timechart: count of `failures_24h` by `source_node` when preprocessed with `timechart` on `last_failure`. (4) Optional sparkline: `last_duration_sec` for migrations over 30 minutes to catch slow network-bound transfers. (5) Map or topology panel: optional; not required. (6) **Drill** token passes `source_node`+`vmid`+`last_upid` into a search filtered to the failure window with `proxmox:task:qmigrate` and optional append of access logs.

references.title: Proxmox VE API Viewer (cluster and node task families)
Proxmox VE Admin Guide — chapter QEMU/KVM virtual machines
Proxmox VE pveum — user and token management
Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (syslog and UF reference)

knownFalsePositives: • (a) A change window where the migration VLAN is renumbered: one or two `network_disconnect` rows in the hour are expected; require three or more in 24h on the same `source_node`+`vmid` or a second corroborating tap on the inter-node link before a hardware RMA. • (b) A GPU or FPGA lab guest that always carries `pci_passthrough_present`—treat the first failure as an inventory truth, not a new incident each time; carry a `no_live_migrate` flag in `proxmox_vm_inventory` and route to an informational queue. • (c) A deliberately attached ISO in a test cluster to prove the `iso_attached` bucket: the alert is the test passing, not a production disk fault. • (d) A cancelled migration from the UI mid-flight: PVE may emit a `status` that reads like a failure even though the operator meant to stop; read `user@pam` in `initiated_by` and compare to the access log before paging application teams. • (e) A forwarder or indexer clock skew that makes `starttime` disagree with `last_failure` in Splunk: the failure row is real but cross-check `last_upid` in the PVE UI before a bridge about “slow migration.” • (f) A duplicate `upid` after forwarder reinstall or a heavy replay of a JSON file: dedupe in alert post-processing on `last_upid`+`source_node`+`_cd` to avoid a duplicate page. • (g) A single `excessive_dirty_pages` during a one-off memory-intensive batch that succeeds on the second `qmigrate` after bandwidth adjustment—`medium` with operator acknowledgement is often enough, not a recurring storm. • (h) A maintenance flag that suppresses `target_resource_exhausted` on a target you intentionally overfilled to prove consolidation limits—this should stay in a lab `index` only.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Proxmox VE 7.x or 8.x cluster with QEMU/KVM guests where live migration (online `qmigrate`) is part of runbooks, maintenance, or host drain.
• A Splunk Universal or Heavy Forwarder with HTTPS to the PVE API (port 8006) or, if you only file-monitor, read access to `/var/log/pveproxy/access.log` and task paths on each node. Python 3 for the API poller.
• Search-time access to `index=virtualization` (or your standard) and a role that can schedule alerts over 24h windows.
• API token for a least-privilege user: `VM.Migrate` (or equivalent for migration), `Sys.Audit` (cluster task visibility), and `Datastore.Audit` if you enrich with storage context. The poller should emit one minified JSON line per task row from `GET /api2/json/cluster/tasks` with `typefilter=qmigrate` and `source=archive` where your build supports historical rows.
• Optional: a `lookups/proxmox_vm_inventory.csv` keyed on `vmid` for `owner_team`, `sla_class`, and `pci_passthrough_devices` so you can post-filter alerts (not in the base SPL) or enrich dashboards.


## Step 1 Onboard Proxmox Migration Telemetry

You need a primary structured feed and a useful secondary for attribution.

Path A (recommended): REST API task polling. The cluster task list returns per-row `upid`, `type` (here `qmigrate`), `status` (`OK`, `running`, or an error string), `node` (source), `id` (vmid as string), `user`, and epoch `starttime` / `endtime` when the task is terminal. The poller should call a URL shaped like `https://<cluster-or-node>:8006/api2/json/cluster/tasks?typefilter=qmigrate&limit=500` and optionally `source=archive` so completed failures are not dropped after rotation. If your security model forbids cluster-wide calls, run the same `typefilter` per node: `/api2/json/nodes/<name>/tasks`. Emit each `data[]` object as a single line to stdout with `sourcetype=proxmox:task:qmigrate` and set `_time` from `starttime` when present.

The Python poller (pattern matches UC-19.1.40 style) should map API keys faithfully: if the JSON includes a `target` or target node in nested form, flatten it to a top-level `target_node` field in the printed line so the SPL `values(target_node)` step is populated; if the API only exposes destination in a detail fetch, the minimum viable poller can leave `target_node` null and still classify failures from `status` text.

Path B: `monitor` on `/var/log/pveproxy/access.log` with the stanza below. This captures who called `POST` against `/nodes/<src>/qemu/<vmid>/migrate` and query parameters, but it does not replace Path A for machine-readable `status` and duration. Use B to enrich operator narratives (“initiated from UI session X”) or to detect migration attempts that never created a resolvable task row in your poll window.

Path C (optional): forward per-task on-disk files under `/var/log/pve/tasks/*/<UPID>` if your packaging can tag a stable sourcetype; multiline text then needs separate props (see Step 2). Most teams start with A plus B.

`inputs.conf` (adjust `$SPLUNK_HOME` and paths; Heavy Forwarder is typical when tokens live in a credential store the UF cannot read):

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/local/inputs.conf
# Heavy Forwarder polling cluster API (or per-node UF if scoping to one node)

[script:///opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/bin/poll_qmigrate_tasks.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 180
sourcetype = proxmox:task:qmigrate
index = virtualization
source = proxmox:rest_api:tasks:qmigrate

[monitor:///var/log/pveproxy/access.log]
disabled = 0
sourcetype = proxmox:pveproxy:access
index = virtualization
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

Wrapper `poll_qmigrate_tasks.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
PVE_API_TOKEN=$(/opt/splunk/bin/splunk show passwd -auth admin:changeme 2>/dev/null | grep proxmox_api_token | awk '{print $NF}')
PVE_API_HOST="pve-cluster.internal:8006"
export PVE_API_TOKEN PVE_API_HOST
exec /usr/bin/python3 /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/bin/poll_qmigrate_tasks.py
```

The companion `poll_qmigrate_tasks.py` should: load token id and secret (prefer a file with mode 0600, not a world-readable env var in production), request tasks with the filters above, iterate `data`, print `json.dumps(row, separators=(',',':'))` per row, and exit non-zero on HTTP errors so ExecProcessor backoffs are visible. Do not invent a `task_type` field; rely on `type` from PVE. Document TLS: either trust your internal CA in `splunkd` or pin `PVE_API_HOST` to a name that matches the cert.

`inputs.conf` optional stanza for task index directory (if you add later):

```ini
# Example only; requires props for multiline task logs, not the primary JSON line contract
# [monitor:///var/log/pve/tasks/...]
# disabled = 1
# sourcetype = proxmox:task:log
```

Token example (operational, not copy-paste secrets): `pveum user add splunk@pve`, `pveum aclmod` with `PVEVMUser` or a custom role that includes migration read rights, `pveum user token add splunk@pve splunk-poll` with the header form `PVEAPIToken user@realm!tokenid=secret`. Rotate tokens on the same schedule as other break-glass credentials.


## Step 2 Parse and Normalize

`props.conf` below keeps one JSON line per event, binds `_time` to the API `starttime` in epoch seconds, and states that multiline task files are a different sourcetype if you add them. The reason we `coalesce(source_node, node, host)` is that the REST row names the source as `node`, some forwarder transforms rename it, and a minority of ad hoc scripts preface `source_node` before forwarding. The guest id is `coalesce(vmid, id)` because the task object stores vmid in `id` for several Proxmox versions.

`props.conf` for the JSON line stream:

```ini
[proxmox:task:qmigrate]
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = none
TIME_PREFIX = "starttime"\s*:\s*
TIME_FORMAT = %s
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
category = Structured
description = Proxmox VE QEMU live-migration task records polled from /api2/json/cluster/tasks?typefilter=qmigrate

[proxmox:pveproxy:access]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
TIME_PREFIX = ^\S+\s+\S+\s+
TIME_FORMAT = [%d/%b/%Y:%H:%M:%S %z]
EXTRACT-vmid = /qemu/(?<vmid>\d+)/migrate
EXTRACT-target_node = target=(?<target_node>[A-Za-z0-9_\-]+)
description = Proxmox pveproxy HTTP access log; used to track who initiated each migration
```

The `case()` chain on `status_norm` maps the lowercased PVE `status` string to stable buckets: CPU heterogeneity, local disk paths without shared or replicated storage, migration channel loss, auto-converge and downtime pressure, target RAM or disk full, migration TCP/SSH range blocked, QMP parameter failures, ISO attached, SR-IOV or PCI passthrough, corosync or quorum, generic timeouts, and a residual `other_error`. Tuning: after each major PVE upgrade, take ten real failure strings from `/cluster/tasks` and extend the `match()` alternations—do not treat the list as closed.

Lookup table for business context (register in `transforms.conf`):

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/lookups/proxmox_vm_inventory.csv
vmid,vm_name,owner_team,sla_class,allowed_target_nodes,pci_passthrough_devices,critical_business_app
100,db-prod-01,db-platform,gold,"pve01,pve02,pve03",,yes
101,oltp-cache-01,app-eng,gold,"pve01,pve02",,yes
102,gpu-ml-01,ml-team,silver,"pve04",nvidia_h100,no
103,jenkins-01,ci-platform,bronze,"pve02,pve03",,no
```

After `stats`, `severity_bucket` encodes: chronic configuration classes (`cpu_flag_mismatch`, `pci_passthrough_present`) as critical, repeated network disconnects, or target resource and cluster membership stress as high, and single-event cases as medium or low. This matches operator intuition: a passthrough GPU guest cannot be live-migrated without architecture change, while a one-off timeout after a long convergence often clears on retry.


## Step 3 Validate

These checks are specific to QEMU online migration task telemetry, not generic Splunk health.

(a) On a shell with `pvesh` or `curl` against the API, run `pvesh get /cluster/tasks --typefilter qmigrate` (flags vary by version) or `GET /api2/json/cluster/tasks?typefilter=qmigrate&limit=20` and confirm the JSON `data` array includes fields this UC expects, especially a recent `UPID:...:qmigrate:...` when someone migrated a test VM. Compare `starttime` and `endtime` to the PVE UI at Datacenter — Tasks with the same `upid`.

(b) Known-bad migration (lab): attach a local ISO to a test VM, for example `qm set <vmid> --ide2 local:iso/proxmox-ve_*.iso,media=cdrom`, then `qm migrate <vmid> <target_node> --online` (or the GUI equivalent). PVE should fail quickly with language matching `iso_attached` or “local CD-ROM” in the task `status` string. Your next poller window should show a non-OK row with `reason_norm=iso_attached` after the SPL filters.

(c) Splunk: `index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:qmigrate" earliest=-1h | head 5` — expand the fields: `source_node` or `node`, `vmid` or `id`, `status`, `upid` must be visible if props and the poller are correct.

(d) If you also ingest pveproxy access, run a second search on `sourcetype=proxmox:pveproxy:access` in the same window and join informally on `vmid` to see initiator source addresses versus API task `user`.

(e) Per-task log deep dive: for one failing `upid`, `GET /api2/json/nodes/<node>/tasks/<URL-encoded-UPID>/log` and confirm the `t` text lines line up in time with the `status` string you class—this is your escape hatch if `status` is shorter than the log.

(f) Negative control: migrate a small idle VM with shared storage and matching CPU types between two healthy nodes; the row should end `OK` and disappear from the failure-oriented SPL because `status_norm=ok` is filtered out.

(g) Quorum: if you maintain a lab where you can stop corosync on one non-active member safely, a refused migration may surface as `corosync_unhealthy` or membership strings—treat that as a validation of the bucket, not a production drill without change approval.


## Step 4 Operationalize

(1) Operator workflow when an alert fires: open the PVE web UI, Datacenter — Tasks, paste the `last_upid` from the alert into the filter, and read the full status line. Identify `source_node` and `vmid` from the table. Check free RAM and pressure on the destination with `pvesh get /nodes/<dst>/status` (or the GUI node summary) before re-trying. If `cpu_flag_mismatch` appears, open `/etc/pve/qemu-server/<vmid>.conf` on a cluster editor session and read the `cpu:` line: heterogenous EPYC vs Xeon clusters often need a conservative model such as `kvm64` for movable guests, which is a change request, not a silent toggle. If `local_storage_unreachable` appears, review whether the guest uses local ZFS with `--with-local-disks` and whether `zfs send/recv` or pvesr alignment exists—cross-check UC-19.1.40 for storage replication, not Ceph health (UC-19.1.39).

(2) Escalation: repeated `cpu_flag_mismatch` across many `vmid`s triggers a platform-eng review of cluster CPU feature policies and golden images. `pci_passthrough_present` or `qmp_command_failed` with SR-IOV in the inventory should route to the application owner: live migration is not a supported path until devices are removed or replaced with paravirt equivalents. `corosync_unhealthy` with concurrent cluster alerts pages the cluster admin before storage or VM teams re-attempt large-scale evacuations. Use Pager throttles: one open incident per `source_node`+`vmid` per 30 minutes unless `severity_bucket=critical`, where immediate bridge is allowed.

(3) Silencing and drilldown: honor maintenance windows with a `maintenance_flag` on nodes from your change system; when set, demote non-critical buckets to a ticket. Keep ISO attach failures unsilenced in production tiers unless the guest is a known “golden” image lab. The drilldown URL pattern is `https://<source_node_fqdn>:8006/?#v1:0:tasks` (name resolution must match the cert). Pass `earliest` from `last_failure` in the tokenized child search. Optional: a dashboard panel that `append`s `sourcetype=proxmox:pveproxy:access` with the same `vmid` in the 15 minutes before `starttime` to show the initiating client.


## Step 5 Continuous Improvement

(1) Tune `severity_bucket` with a clone search that prepends `streamstats` over 7 days by `source_node` and `vmid` to count streaks of `other_error` after maintenance—if a guest always fails once then succeeds, widen the `medium` window or add a `dedup` on `last_upid`. Pair with a second scheduled search: `| bin _time span=1h | stats count by source_node, reason_norm` to find chronic patterns during backup hours or batch jobs that dirty memory.

(2) Enrichment: call `/api2/json/cluster/resources?type=node` in a separate poller and `lookup` memory pressure: if a `target_resource_exhausted` failure lands when the target is at 90% RAM by design (dense GPU nodes), route to capacity review rather than an immediate re-page. For SOAR, you might script a one-time `qm set` CPU model after explicit approval—never auto-change production `cpu` without a ticket, but a lab can demonstrate an automated `kvm64` retry once.

(3) LXC: `lxcmigrate` (restart-based) is a different `type`—either extend the poller to emit `sourcetype=proxmox:task:lxcmigrate` with parallel SPL, or keep this UC strictly QEMU and document the gap in `exclusions`.

(4) Drift: Proxmox can add post-copy or bandwidth strings—revisit the `excessive_dirty_pages` bucket when you enable new QEMU capabilities.

**Primary saved search (must match the `spl` field in this JSON file character-for-character):**

```spl
index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:qmigrate" earliest=-24h@h
| eval source_node = coalesce(source_node, node, host)
| eval vmid = coalesce(vmid, id)
| eval status_norm = lower(coalesce(status, ""))
| where status_norm != "ok" AND status_norm != "running" AND isnotnull(status)
| eval reason_norm = case(
    match(status_norm, "(target host cpu does not support|cpu (model|feature)|incompatible cpu)"), "cpu_flag_mismatch",
    match(status_norm, "(could not migrate.*local|local-disk|local disk image|storage.*not (shared|available))"), "local_storage_unreachable",
    match(status_norm, "(channel disconnected|connection reset|network unreachable|migration network)"), "network_disconnect",
    match(status_norm, "(downtime|migration speed too slow|excessive (dirty|memory)|auto.?converge.*failed)"), "excessive_dirty_pages",
    match(status_norm, "(insufficient (memory|ram)|out of memory|storage.*full|max.*concurrent.*migrations)"), "target_resource_exhausted",
    match(status_norm, "(firewall|port.*blocked|connection refused.*60[0-9]{3})"), "firewall_blocked",
    match(status_norm, "(qmp command.*failed|migrate.set.capabilities|migrate.set.parameters)"), "qmp_command_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(local cd.?rom|iso (image|attached)|cdrom is on local)"), "iso_attached",
    match(status_norm, "(pci.?(e)? passthrough|hostpci|sr.?iov)"), "pci_passthrough_present",
    match(status_norm, "(corosync|quorum|cluster membership)"), "corosync_unhealthy",
    match(status_norm, "got timeout|operation timed out|deadline exceeded"), "timeout",
    1==1, "other_error")
| eval duration_sec = if(isnotnull(endtime) AND isnotnull(starttime), endtime - starttime, null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_failure,
        count as failures_24h,
        values(reason_norm) as reasons,
        values(status) as raw_status_msgs,
        values(target_node) as target_nodes,
        latest(duration_sec) as last_duration_sec,
        latest(upid) as last_upid,
        latest(user) as initiated_by
        by source_node, vmid
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    mvfind(reasons, "cpu_flag_mismatch") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "pci_passthrough_present") >= 0, "critical",
    failures_24h >= 3 AND mvfind(reasons, "network_disconnect") >= 0, "high",
    mvfind(reasons, "target_resource_exhausted") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "corosync_unhealthy") >= 0, "high",
    failures_24h >= 1, "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| sort -failures_24h, +severity_bucket
| table last_failure, source_node, target_nodes, vmid, severity_bucket, failures_24h, reasons, raw_status_msgs, last_duration_sec, initiated_by, last_upid
```

Narrative for reviewers: the `where` clause removes healthy `ok` and in-flight `running` task rows so the alert focuses on failed or anomalous terminal states. The `stats` by `source_node` and `vmid` groups retries in the 24h window, `mvfind` is used to scan multivalue `reasons` for classification, and the final `table` is suitable for a dashboard, email, and on-call handoff with `last_upid` as the key back into PVE. For drilldowns, map `source_node` to a DNS name your browser trusts.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for times when a running computer in your farm is being slid from one machine to another and that move does not finish. If that move fails in secret, the team may think the guest already left the old room when it did not, so we call it out while the window to try again is still open.
hardware: Typical 1U/2U Dell PowerEdge, HPE ProLiant, or Supermicro nodes running Proxmox VE 7/8+ with two sockets (Intel Xeon Scalable or AMD EPYC) where matching `cpu` models across nodes matters for `qmigrate`, plus 64–768 GB RAM per host for guest working sets. Live migration pre-copy memory uses the migration network defined in `datacenter.cfg` (`migration: type=secure,network=10.0.0.0/24` is common) on dedicated 10/25/100 Gbps NICs separate from front-door VM access VLANs. Shared storage may be Ceph RBD, NFS, or iSCSI; local ZFS with `--with-local-disks` implies ZFS send/recv and bandwidth headroom. Firewall paths must allow the documented migration port range (often TCP/UDP 60000–60050) or SSH tunnelling when `type=secure` is set. Heterogeneous CPU clusters may require conservative models (`kvm64`) in `/etc/pve/qemu-server/<vmid>.conf` for online moves to succeed.
